news_summary: Positive environmental developments include thriving marine life boosting whale populations, innovative recycling of old electrical cables, and sustainable forest management enhancing water resources.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les développements environnementaux positifs, on peut citer la prospérité de la vie marine qui favorise la croissance des populations de baleines, le recyclage innovant des vieux câbles électriques et la gestion durable des forêts qui améliore les ressources en eau.
news_summary_es: Entre los avances medioambientales positivos se incluyen el auge de la vida marina, que ha impulsado el aumento de las poblaciones de ballenas, el reciclaje innovador de cables eléctricos viejos y la gestión forestal sostenible, que ha mejorado los recursos hídricos.

articles.title: Humpback whale recovery is changing who fathers the calves
articles.summary: A new study shows that as humpback whale populations recover from past whaling, older males are gaining a major advantage in reproduction. Early in the recovery, breeding groups were dominated by younger whales. But as more mature males returned, they increasingly fathered more calves than their younger rivals. Scientists say experience in singing and competing may help older males win the breeding battle.
articles.content:
A new study from the University of St Andrews, published on February 27 in Current Biology, shows that the influence of age on male humpback whale reproduction has shifted as whale populations rebound after centuries of commercial whaling.

Whaling once pushed many large whale species to the edge of extinction. Although hunting has long since stopped, its effects did not end with declining numbers. Even decades later, the legacy of whaling continues to shape whale populations, affecting not only how many whales exist today but also which males succeed in fathering calves.

Two Decades of Data From South Pacific Whales

Researchers analyzed nearly 20 years of data from humpback whales that breed in the waters around New Caledonia in the South Pacific. The study relied on long term monitoring carried out by the NGO Opération Cétacés. Using this extensive dataset, scientists investigated how the population's age structure, male behavior, and paternity patterns have changed over time.

During the early years of population recovery, breeding groups were largely made up of younger males. As whale numbers increased, the population gradually developed a more balanced mix of younger and older individuals. As older whales became more common, they also became more successful at fathering calves than their younger competitors.

Genetic Tools Reveal Hidden Fathers

Humpback whales have never been directly observed mating in the wild, which means the identity of a calf's father has historically been unknown.

To solve this mystery, the international research team led by the Sea Mammal Research Unit at the University of St Andrews used genetic testing to determine paternity. They also used a technique known as an 'epigenetic molecular clock' to estimate the age of individual whales. Both pieces of information could be obtained from a small skin sample collected from the animals.

Whale Songs and Competition for Mates

Male humpback whales are famous for producing some of the most elaborate songs in the animal kingdom. These powerful vocal performances can carry across large areas of the breeding grounds and are believed to play an important role in attracting females. Males may also accompany females closely or compete directly with rival males in intense physical encounters.

Senior author Dr. Ellen Garland of the Sea Mammal Research Unit explained, "Mating behavior, and who was successful at mating, changed with these shifts in age structure. As the population recovered, there were more older males than expected singing, escorting females, and successfully fathering calves compared to younger animals."

The results suggest that male humpback whales likely need years to develop and perfect both their songs and their competitive strategies. This experience may give older whales a clear advantage when competing for mates. As populations continue to grow, females may also become more selective, which could further favor males with stronger displays or other desirable traits.

The Long Shadow of Whaling on Whale Behavior

The findings highlight the importance of continued research on recovering whale populations. Long term studies help scientists understand how past exploitation continues to influence population structure, competition, and reproduction.

The research also points to a broader issue in whale science. Much of what scientists know about whale behavior comes from studying populations that had already been heavily impacted by whaling. In other words, researchers have been working from a shifted baseline rather than observing completely undisturbed populations.

Dr. Franca Eichenberger, lead author of the study from the Sea Mammal Research Unit at the University of St Andrews, said, "It is only now, as whale populations recover and new analytical tools become available, that we are beginning to understand how far-reaching the consequences of whaling truly are. The impacts extend beyond population size -- they shape behavior, competition, and reproduction."

Dr. Eichenberger added, "Virtually all populations of whales have changed due to whaling; our work shows that they continue to change as they recover. This is why the continued long-term monitoring of previously exploited whale populations is so important. Humpback whales have shown a remarkable comeback over recent years. Now is the time we can learn so much more about their behavior and life history. We just need to keep looking."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the significant recovery of humpback whale populations after centuries of commercial whaling, highlighting a positive ecological impact with broad significance for marine biodiversity. It provides detailed scientific findings on how population recovery influences whale behavior and reproduction, illustrating a meaningful environmental success story with substantial context and implications for conservation.
articles.personality_title: Older humpback whales gain an edge as populations recover
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Humpback whales were hunted almost to extinction by whalers for hundreds of years. Although whaling has stopped, the effects of this hunting still affect how whale populations grow and behave today.

**What happened** – Scientists from the University of St Andrews studied humpback whales near New Caledonia in the South Pacific. Using nearly 20 years of data and genetic tests, they found that as whale numbers increased, older male whales began fathering more calves than younger males. This shows a change in which whales are more successful at reproducing.

**Impact** – The study reveals that older males have an advantage because they are more experienced at singing and competing for mates. Whale songs help attract females, and older whales seem better at this. This change in breeding patterns shows how whale populations are not just growing but also changing in behavior as they recover.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to monitor whale populations to learn more about how recovery affects their behavior and reproduction. This ongoing study helps us understand the long-term effects of human impact and how animals adjust over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – As humpback whale numbers recover, older males are increasingly successful fathers, showing how population growth can change animal behavior over time.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les baleines à bosse plus âgées prennent l'avantage avec la reprise des populations
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les baleines à bosse ont été chassées presque jusqu'à l'extinction pendant des centaines d'années. Même si la chasse est arrêtée, ses effets influencent encore aujourd'hui la croissance et le comportement des populations.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques de l'université de St Andrews ont étudié les baleines à bosse près de la Nouvelle-Calédonie dans le Pacifique Sud. Grâce à près de 20 ans de données et des tests génétiques, ils ont découvert qu'à mesure que le nombre de baleines augmentait, les mâles plus âgés avaient plus de petits que les plus jeunes.

**Impact** – L'étude montre que les mâles plus âgés ont un avantage car ils sont plus expérimentés pour chanter et rivaliser pour les femelles. Ces chants attirent les femelles, et les mâles plus âgés sont meilleurs à cela. Ce changement dans la reproduction montre que les populations ne grandissent pas seulement, elles changent aussi de comportement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à surveiller les populations de baleines pour mieux comprendre comment la reprise influence leur comportement et leur reproduction. Cette étude aide à comprendre les effets à long terme des activités humaines.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Avec la reprise des baleines à bosse, les mâles plus âgés réussissent davantage à se reproduire, montrant comment la croissance des populations peut changer le comportement animal.
articles.personality_title_es: Las ballenas jorobadas mayores ganan ventaja con la recuperación de la población
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las ballenas jorobadas fueron cazadas casi hasta la extinción durante cientos de años. Aunque la caza terminó, sus efectos todavía influyen en cómo crecen y se comportan las poblaciones de ballenas hoy.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de St Andrews estudiaron ballenas jorobadas cerca de Nueva Caledonia en el Pacífico Sur. Usando casi 20 años de datos y pruebas genéticas, descubrieron que a medida que aumentaba el número de ballenas, los machos mayores tenían más crías que los más jóvenes.

**Impacto** – El estudio muestra que los machos mayores tienen ventaja porque son más experimentados en cantar y competir por las hembras. Las canciones atraen a las hembras, y los machos mayores son mejores en esto. Este cambio en la reproducción muestra que las poblaciones no solo crecen, sino que también cambian su comportamiento.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán observando las poblaciones para entender mejor cómo la recuperación afecta su comportamiento y reproducción. Este estudio ayuda a comprender los efectos a largo plazo de la actividad humana.

**Resumen en una frase** – Con la recuperación de las ballenas jorobadas, los machos mayores tienen más éxito en ser padres, mostrando cómo el crecimiento de la población puede cambiar el comportamiento animal.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a serene South Pacific seascape at dawn, featuring a group of humpback whales of varying ages gracefully swimming and singing together, with gentle ripples symbolizing the passage of time and recovery, their majestic forms illuminated softly against a calm ocean and pastel sky.

articles.title: Ailing “Megaberg” Sparks Surge of Microscopic Life
articles.summary: As Iceberg A-23A disintegrated, it shed meltwater that helped fuel an extensive phytoplankton bloom in the South Atlantic Ocean.
articles.content:
Natural color Chlorophyll NASA Earth Observatory NASA Earth Observatory Natural color Chlorophyll NASA Earth Observatory NASA Earth Observatory Natural color Chlorophyll January 25, 2026 Curtain Toggle 2-Up

Iceberg A-23A has had a more eventful run than most of the large Antarctic icebergs that have calved from the continent's ice shelves in recent decades. Over its winding, forty-plus-year journey, the "megaberg" spent decades grounded in the Weddell Sea before drifting north, twirling in an ocean vortex for months, and nearly colliding with an island in 2025.

By 2026, the iconic iceberg, sopping with meltwater and shedding smaller bergs as it moved into warmer ocean waters, put on one more show. The chunks of ice and frigid glacial meltwater left in its wake appear to have fueled a surge in phytoplankton abundance, known as a bloom, observed in surface waters by NASA satellites.

Phytoplankton, which harvest sunlight to carry out photosynthesis, form the base of the marine food web. They also produce up to half of the oxygen on Earth and serve as part of the ocean’s “biological carbon pump,” which transfers carbon dioxide from the atmosphere to the deep ocean.

The VIIRS (Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite) on the Suomi NPP satellite captured this image (left) of the splintering tabular berg on January 25, 2026. The image was acquired after several large pieces had drifted northwestward and then curled toward the northeast following the iceberg breaking apart on January 9. A debris field full of brash ice, small icebergs, and bergy bits was visible east of the largest remaining pieces. Also on January 25, the OCI (Ocean Color Instrument) on NASA’s PACE (Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, Ocean Ecosystem) satellite detected plumes of chlorophyll-a (right) drifting around the remaining bergs and debris field. Researchers use chlorophyll concentrations as a marker of phytoplankton abundance.

January 25, 2026

"This bloom is too big and too clearly spreading from the icebergs not to be strongly linked to them," said Grant Bigg, an emeritus oceanographer at the University of Sheffield. Bigg, who has studied how large icebergs have enhanced phytoplankton activity in this region, noted that while blooms unconnected to icebergs do occur regularly here, satellite imagery shows a connection that has persisted for weeks—increasing his confidence that the iceberg and phytoplankton bloom are related.

The primary factors that limit phytoplankton in this region are access to light and nutrients, explained Heidi Dierssen, an oceanographer at the University of Connecticut. Light can be limiting even in the summer because phytoplankton are often mixed too deeply in the water column due to high winds and turbulence.

Melting icebergs can boost phytoplankton by both creating a stable surface layer with favorable growth conditions and releasing plumes of meltwater rich in iron—a key nutrient for phytoplankton that can be scarce in this part of the South Atlantic, she said. Research indicates that icebergs also often contain significant amounts of manganese and macronutrients, such as nitrates and phosphates, that can benefit phytoplankton. These nutrients often accumulate on icebergs when they were part of the larger ice sheet through windblown dust or through contact with bedrock or soil.

The Landsat 8 image above, captured by the OLI (Operational Land Imager) on January 25, 2026, shows blue meltwater pooling on several of the larger fragments. The linear patterns are likely related to striations that were etched hundreds of years ago when the ice was part of a glacier moving across Antarctic bedrock. Dark staining, perhaps cryoconite dust, is visible on some of the bergs.

Bigg also noted that the phytoplankton signal appears to be more concentrated near the smaller bergs, possibly because these are melting faster, releasing nutrient-rich material at a higher rate. Dierssen added that it's also possible that chlorophyll concentrations may be higher near the largest bergs than they appear because algorithms sometimes overcorrect for "adjacency effects" near bright surfaces, like ice, when processing chlorophyll data.

Ivona Cetinić, a researcher on NASA's PACE science team, checked a database for clues about the smallest, or "pico," phytoplankton swirling around the bergs. The tool, called MOANA (Multiple Ordination ANAlysis), taps into hyperspectral satellite observations of ocean color from PACE.

MOANA indicated that picoeukaryotic phytoplankton—microscopic eukaryotic organisms that respond quickly to changes in temperature or nutrient availability—were thriving in these waters when the image was captured. The swirls to the west of the berg were made of a slightly larger group of cyanobacteria called Synechococcus, she said. The PACE team is currently developing additional tools that will help identify communities of larger types of phytoplankton, which were likely present as well.

Some research suggests that icebergs may have contributed significantly to phytoplankton blooms in this region in recent years, possibly accounting for up to one-fifth of the Southern Ocean's total carbon sequestration. Other research teams have concluded that surface waters trailing icebergs were about one-third more likely to have increased amounts of phytoplankton compared to background levels.

How long Iceberg A-23A will enhance phytoplankton productivity before and after disintegrating completely remains an open question. NASA scientists watching the berg say it continued to shrink and shed mass in February, but as of March 3, 2026, it remained just slightly above the size threshold required for naming and tracking by the U.S. National Ice Center.

Past research indicates that icebergs can sustain elevated chlorophyll concentrations for more than a month after passing through in trails that stretch for hundreds of kilometers. Icebergs and the blooms surrounding them have also been known to attract fish, seabirds, and other types of marine life, highlighting the important ecological role they play.

NASA Earth Observatory images by Michala Garrison, using VIIRS data from NASA EOSDIS LANCE, GIBS/Worldview, and the Suomi National Polar-orbiting Partnership, PACE data from the NASA Ocean Biology Distributed Active Archive Center OB.DAAC, and Landsat data from the U.S. Geological Survey. Story by Adam Voiland.

Downloads Suomi NPP, January 25, 2026 PACE, January 25, 2026 Landsat, January 25, 2026

References & Resources
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant natural event where the disintegration of a large Antarctic iceberg (A-23A) has led to a substantial phytoplankton bloom in the South Atlantic Ocean. This bloom has broad environmental significance because phytoplankton produce a large portion of Earth's oxygen and play a key role in carbon sequestration, benefiting global climate regulation and marine ecosystems. The story provides detailed scientific context, satellite observations, and expert insights, demonstrating a meaningful ecological impact with measurable benefits to the environment and marine life.
articles.personality_title: Melting Antarctic iceberg sparks burst of ocean life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Iceberg A-23A is a huge piece of ice that broke off Antarctica over 40 years ago. It has traveled through the ocean, melting slowly as it moves into warmer waters.

**What happened** – In early 2026, this iceberg started breaking apart, releasing cold meltwater into the South Atlantic Ocean. Scientists using NASA satellites saw a big increase in tiny ocean plants called phytoplankton growing near the melting ice.

**Impact** – Phytoplankton are important because they produce much of the Earth's oxygen and help remove carbon dioxide from the air. The meltwater from the iceberg provided nutrients like iron and other minerals that helped the phytoplankton grow quickly. This bloom of life supports the ocean food chain and helps the planet’s climate.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue watching the iceberg and the phytoplankton bloom using satellites. They want to learn how long the bloom lasts and how it affects marine animals like fish and seabirds. This information can improve our understanding of how melting ice affects ocean ecosystems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The melting of Iceberg A-23A has fueled a large growth of tiny ocean plants, showing how icebergs support marine life and help the planet’s climate.
articles.personality_title_fr: La fonte d’un iceberg antarctique déclenche une explosion de vie océanique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’iceberg A-23A est un énorme morceau de glace qui s’est détaché de l’Antarctique il y a plus de 40 ans. Il a voyagé dans l’océan en fondant lentement en approchant des eaux plus chaudes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Au début de 2026, cet iceberg a commencé à se fragmenter, libérant de l’eau de fonte froide dans l’océan Atlantique Sud. Des scientifiques utilisant des satellites de la NASA ont observé une forte augmentation de petites plantes marines appelées phytoplancton près de la glace fondante.

**Impact** – Le phytoplancton est important car il produit une grande partie de l’oxygène de la Terre et aide à retirer le dioxyde de carbone de l’air. L’eau de fonte de l’iceberg a apporté des nutriments comme le fer qui ont favorisé la croissance rapide du phytoplancton. Cette prolifération soutient la chaîne alimentaire marine et aide le climat de la planète.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront de surveiller l’iceberg et la floraison de phytoplancton avec les satellites. Ils veulent comprendre combien de temps dure cette floraison et comment elle influence les poissons et les oiseaux marins. Ces études amélioreront la connaissance de l’impact de la fonte des glaces sur les écosystèmes marins.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La fonte de l’iceberg A-23A a favorisé une grande croissance de minuscules plantes océaniques, montrant comment les icebergs soutiennent la vie marine et le climat terrestre.
articles.personality_title_es: El derretimiento de un iceberg antártico impulsa un auge de vida oceánica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El iceberg A-23A es un gran bloque de hielo que se desprendió de la Antártida hace más de 40 años. Ha viajado por el océano, derritiéndose lentamente al acercarse a aguas más cálidas.

**Qué pasó** – A principios de 2026, este iceberg comenzó a fragmentarse, liberando agua de deshielo fría en el Océano Atlántico Sur. Científicos con satélites de la NASA observaron un gran aumento de pequeñas plantas marinas llamadas fitoplancton cerca del hielo que se estaba derritiendo.

**Impacto** – El fitoplancton es importante porque produce gran parte del oxígeno de la Tierra y ayuda a eliminar dióxido de carbono del aire. El agua de deshielo del iceberg aportó nutrientes como hierro que facilitaron el rápido crecimiento del fitoplancton. Esta floración apoya la cadena alimentaria marina y ayuda al clima del planeta.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán observando el iceberg y la floración de fitoplancton con satélites. Quieren saber cuánto tiempo durará la floración y cómo afecta a peces y aves marinas. Esto ayudará a entender mejor cómo el derretimiento de hielo afecta a los ecosistemas oceánicos.

**Resumen en una frase** – El derretimiento del iceberg A-23A ha impulsado un gran crecimiento de plantas oceánicas diminutas, mostrando cómo los icebergs apoyan la vida marina y el clima del planeta.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a large, melting Antarctic iceberg drifting through deep blue ocean waters, surrounded by swirling vibrant green and teal plumes symbolizing abundant phytoplankton blooms, with smaller ice fragments trailing behind and subtle hints of sunlight filtering through the water to evoke life and renewal.

articles.title: What Can You Do With Your Old Electrical Wires and Cables?
articles.summary:
Do you have a box of old electrical wires and cables lying in a corner...
The post What Can You Do With Your Old Electrical Wires and Cables? appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Do you have a box of old electrical wires and cables lying in a corner of your house? You’re not alone. According to the UN’s Global E-waste Monitor 2024, a record 62 million metric tons of electronic waste was generated worldwide in 2022, an 82% increase since 2010. E-waste is projected to reach 82 million metric tons by 2030. In the U.S. alone, roughly 8 million tons of e-waste is discarded each year.

Electrical construction materials, including the types of wires and cables used in your home wiring, along with the ever-growing pile of charging cords, USB cables, HDMI cables, and Ethernet cables that accumulate in drawers and boxes, make up a significant portion of this waste. But it’s important to keep electrical wires and cables out of the trash; they contain valuable resources that should be recycled.

The plastic coating on wires and cables, which can cause environmental harm, is commonly made of polyethylene or polyvinyl chloride (PVC). When exposed to extreme heat and other environmental conditions in a landfill, the plastic coating can release harmful contaminants like lead and dioxins into the environment. Power cords and extension cords are classified as “tanglers” by recycling programs, because they can jam sorting equipment, causing worker injuries and costly maintenance. They should never go in your curbside recycling bin.

Fortunately, the options for responsibly disposing of old or unused electrical wires and cables have expanded significantly. Here are seven ways to keep them out of landfills.

Retail Drop-Off Programs

The easiest option for most people is dropping off old wires and cables at a major retailer that accepts e-waste. Several national chains offer convenient recycling programs:

Best Buy remains the nation’s largest retail collector of e-waste, having recycled more than 2.7 billion pounds of electronics and appliances since 2009. Every U.S. Best Buy store has a kiosk just inside the front door where customers can drop off rechargeable batteries, wires, cords, cables, and plastic bags at no cost. You can also bring up to three items per household per day to the customer service counter for free recycling, regardless of where you purchased them. Check Best Buy’s recycling page for state-specific details, as limitations vary.

Staples offers free in-store technology recycling at the checkout counter, covering everything from desktops and laptops to peripherals like keyboards, mice, speakers, and cables. All brands are accepted, and the retailer works exclusively with e-Stewards-certified recyclers, the most rigorous standard for responsible electronics recycling in the U.S. Note that Staples has been closing some locations, so confirm your local store’s availability and accepted items before visiting by checking the Staples Recycling Services page.

Office Depot/OfficeMax offers free in-store recycling for cell phones, rechargeable batteries, and ink and toner cartridges. For other electronics including cables and wires, they sell Tech Recycling Boxes in three sizes ($22.99 for small, $18.29 for medium, $28 for large) that you fill with electronics and return to the store for processing.

Mail-In Recycling Programs

If you don’t live near a retail drop-off location or prefer the convenience of shipping from home, several mail-in options accept wires and cables:

Best Buy Technology Recycling Boxes can be ordered online and shipped to your home. A small box (9x5x3 inches, up to 6 lbs.) costs $22.99 and a medium box (18x14x4 inches, up to 15 lbs.) costs $29.99. Each comes with tape, instructions, and a prepaid UPS shipping label. Fill the box with cables, cords, keyboards, webcams, and other small electronics, then take it to a UPS drop-off location or schedule a pickup. Best Buy’s recycling partner handles data destruction and responsible recycling.

Homeboy Electronics Recycling offers mail-in recycling kits that include prepaid shipping and R2-certified processing. Their accepted items include cords and wire, cable and streaming devices, and a wide range of other electronics. As a social enterprise affiliated with Homeboy Industries, purchasing a kit also supports job training and employment opportunities for formerly incarcerated individuals — making it an option that addresses both environmental and social impact.

Waste Management Recycle by Mail offers recycling kits for electronics, batteries, and bulbs. Enter your address on their website to see which mail-in services are available in your area.

Brand Take-Back Programs

Several major manufacturers offer take-back and recycling programs that accept cables and accessories:

Apple provides free recycling of devices and accessories, including cables, chargers, adapters, cases, and other peripherals, at any Apple Store or by mail. Visit Apple’s Trade-In website to request a prepaid shipping label and send your old accessories at no cost. The company uses advanced disassembly robots to recover valuable materials, such as aluminum, cobalt, and rare-earth elements, for use in new products.

Dell Reconnect, a partnership with Goodwill Industries since 2004, provides free recycling at more than 2,000 participating Goodwill locations across 44 U.S. states. The program accepts any brand of computer equipment and accessories — including cables, keyboards, mice, and speakers — in any condition. Over 20 years, Dell Reconnect has recovered and recycled more than 635 million pounds of electronics. Materials are processed through Dell’s open-loop recycling system, where recovered plastics and metals are reintroduced into new products. Dell also offers a free Mail Back program for consumers who prefer to ship their old electronics directly.

Sell

You may be able to find buyers for your old electrical wires and cables. There are many technology enthusiasts and makers who hang on to old gadgets or develop unique new systems. Your old charging cord may be just the type of cable they’re looking for. Try listing what you have on Craigslist, Facebook Marketplace, or eBay.

Most metal recycling centers accept old wires and cables for the copper and aluminum they contain. Copper, in particular, is highly valuable; roughly $8 billion worth of copper was recycled in the U.S. in 2021. Some recyclers may accept only stripped metal wire, requiring you to remove the plastic insulation first. If you have a substantial quantity of cabling, electrical liquidators may also be interested in purchasing your materials in bulk.

Reuse

Before recycling, consider whether your old wires and cables can be reused. Many charging cords and cables work with multiple gadgets, especially now that the USB-C standard has become widespread across Android phones, tablets, laptops, and other devices (and even Apple’s newest iPhones). Keep a backup charging cord handy, and offer extras to family members or friends.

If you don’t know anyone who needs them, post your cables for free on Freecycle or local Buy Nothing groups on Facebook so someone else can put them to use.

Donate

Schools and nonprofits running STEM programs often welcome donations of cables, chargers, and other electronic accessories for student projects and labs. Local charities, community centers, and shelters may also need working chargers and cords for the people they serve. A quick call to local organizations can help you find recipients who can use what you no longer need.

Recycle Through Local E-Waste Programs

If none of the options above are convenient, check your local municipality’s e-waste recycling program. Many cities and counties operate dedicated e-waste recycling facilities or host periodic collection events — often around Earth Day or during spring-cleaning season.

Some states have enacted specific e-waste legislation that makes recycling easier. Programs like E-Cycle Washington and California’s Covered Electronic Waste Recycling Program offer free drop-off options for residents.

Renew Your eWaste

With less than a quarter of global e-waste being properly collected and recycled, there’s an enormous opportunity for individuals to make a difference. The metals embedded in the world’s annual e-waste, including copper, gold, iron, and rare earth elements, are valued at approximately $91 billion, according to the UN’s E-waste Monitor. Every cable and cord you recycle contributes to recovering these valuable materials rather than burying them in a landfill, and helps prevent toxic substances from contaminating soil and water.

Old wires and cables should never go in the trash or the curbside recycling bin. With retail drop-offs, mail-in programs, brand take-back initiatives, and local e-waste recycling options now widely available, there’s a responsible option for every situation, often at no cost.

Editor’s Note: Originally published on October 15, 2020, this article was most recently updated in February 2026.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant, practical solutions for recycling electrical wires and cables, addressing the growing global e-waste problem with broad environmental benefits. It provides detailed, actionable options for individuals to reduce landfill waste and recover valuable materials, promoting sustainable behavior with measurable positive impact on society and the environment.
articles.personality_title: How to recycle your old electrical wires and cables safely
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Electronic waste, or e-waste, is growing fast around the world. In 2022, people threw away 62 million metric tons of e-waste, including old electrical wires and cables. These items contain valuable metals but can harm the environment if thrown in the trash.

**What happened** – New programs and options have made it easier to recycle old wires and cables. You can take them to stores like Best Buy, Staples, or Office Depot, send them by mail using special kits, or use brand take-back programs from companies like Apple and Dell. You can also sell, reuse, or donate cables instead of throwing them away.

**Impact** – Recycling wires and cables helps recover valuable metals like copper and rare earth elements worth billions of dollars. It also stops harmful plastics and toxins from polluting soil and water. These programs reduce waste in landfills and lower risks to recycling workers.

**What's next step** – More people are encouraged to use these recycling options. Local governments may expand e-waste programs, and companies could improve take-back services. Recycling more cables means less pollution and more materials for new products.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Recycling old electrical wires and cables through available programs keeps valuable materials out of landfills and protects the environment from harmful waste.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment recycler vos vieux fils et câbles électriques en toute sécurité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les déchets électroniques, ou e-déchets, augmentent rapidement dans le monde. En 2022, 62 millions de tonnes ont été jetées, dont des fils et câbles électriques usagés. Ces objets contiennent des métaux précieux, mais peuvent polluer s'ils sont mis à la poubelle.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – De nouveaux programmes facilitent le recyclage des fils et câbles. Vous pouvez les déposer dans des magasins comme Best Buy, Staples ou Office Depot, les envoyer par courrier avec des kits spéciaux, ou utiliser les programmes de reprise de marques comme Apple et Dell. Vous pouvez aussi vendre, réutiliser ou donner ces câbles.

**Impact** – Recycler ces fils permet de récupérer des métaux précieux comme le cuivre et des terres rares, d’une valeur de plusieurs milliards de dollars. Cela empêche aussi les plastiques toxiques de polluer le sol et l'eau. Ces programmes réduisent les déchets et protègent les travailleurs du recyclage.

**Prochaine étape** – Il est important que plus de personnes utilisent ces solutions. Les collectivités locales pourraient étendre les programmes et les entreprises améliorer les services de reprise. Recycler plus de câbles réduit la pollution et fournit des matériaux pour fabriquer de nouveaux produits.

**Phrase clé** – Recycler ses vieux fils et câbles électriques grâce aux programmes existants préserve l’environnement et récupère des matériaux précieux.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo reciclar tus viejos cables y alambres eléctricos de forma segura
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los desechos electrónicos, o e-basura, están creciendo rápidamente en todo el mundo. En 2022, se tiraron 62 millones de toneladas de e-basura, incluyendo cables y alambres eléctricos viejos. Estos contienen metales valiosos pero pueden dañar el medio ambiente si se tiran a la basura.

**Qué pasó** – Nuevos programas y opciones hacen más fácil reciclar cables y alambres viejos. Puedes llevarlos a tiendas como Best Buy, Staples u Office Depot, enviarlos por correo con kits especiales, o usar programas de devolución de marcas como Apple y Dell. También puedes vender, reutilizar o donar los cables.

**Impacto** – Reciclar cables ayuda a recuperar metales valiosos como cobre y tierras raras, valorados en miles de millones de dólares. También evita que los plásticos tóxicos contaminen el suelo y el agua. Estos programas reducen la basura en vertederos y protegen a los trabajadores del reciclaje.

**Próximo paso** – Se anima a más personas a usar estas opciones. Los gobiernos locales podrían ampliar los programas de e-basura y las empresas mejorar los servicios de devolución. Reciclar más cables significa menos contaminación y más materiales para nuevos productos.

**Frase clave** – Reciclar viejos cables y alambres eléctricos con los programas disponibles protege el medio ambiente y recupera materiales valiosos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cozy home corner with neatly coiled colorful electrical wires and cables gently transforming into vibrant green vines that intertwine with glowing recycled metal fragments, symbolizing renewal and responsible e-waste recycling.

articles.title: Why thinning a forest could get you more drinking water
articles.content:
You might appreciate snowpack as something to sled, ski, or snowboard on. But beyond the slopes, vast masses of snow melt as winter turns to spring, feeding rivers and streams, which go on to hydrate towns and cities and crops. We’re talking incredible amounts of water: California, for instance, gets 30 percent of its supply from the snowpack in its Sierra Nevada mountains.

But across the American west, that bounty is in trouble as the climate quickly changes: The region is currently in the grip of a severe snow drought, as more precipitation falls as rain. At the same time, higher temperatures are desiccating the landscape, fueling massive wildfires once all that snow melts away. Not helping matters is a long history of fire suppression — quickly stamping out blazes has allowed dry vegetation to accumulate, adding yet more fuel to the flames.

Scientists seem to have found a way to help alleviate the West’s fire and ice problems simultaneously, at least in Washington state. Working in the forests of the Cascade Mountains, researchers divided plots on the south and north slopes of a ridge and thinned their vegetation to varying degrees: Trees like ponderosa pine and Douglas fir were left different distances apart, for instance, determining how dense the growth could be. (The image above shows what the landscape looked like to start, and the two below show different intensities of thinning.) After the pack formed, they measured the thickness and density of the snow in each plot. That way, they could show the difference between an area where trees were, say, 15 feet apart versus 30 feet, and compare those plots to ones that hadn’t been thinned at all.

Western states will no doubt be interested in what these researchers found: up to 30 percent more snowpack on the thinned plots compared to the areas left unkempt. Scaled up, that would mean an additional 4 million gallons of water per 100 acres of forest. Furthermore, the researchers identified a sweet spot, where foresters would get similar benefits with a tree spacing anywhere between 13 and 52 feet. “It gives foresters a lot more flexibility in how they can manage their trees, depending on what species are there,” said Emily Howe, an aquatic ecologist at the Nature Conservancy, who coauthored a new paper describing the research. (The Conservancy owns the property where the study occurred.)

This density mimics what happened naturally on these landscapes before humans showed up, when vegetation would grow and die back, accumulating as tinder. All it took was one lightning strike to get a conflagration going. “We’re not talking like big, catastrophic wildfires that burn everything in their path, because it happened so frequently,” said Susan Dickerson-Lange, director of the Climate Impacts Group at the University of Washington and coauthor of the paper. “These fires naturally clear out some of the underbrush. They burn some of the trees, but not all of the trees, leaving this very sort of patchy dynamic.”

Scientists compared snowpack in forest areas that underwent moderate thinning (left) compared to heavy thinning (right), which increasingly opened up the landscape. John Marshall

That gentler ecological reset would do two things: Prevent the giant, landscape-obliterating blazes we’re coping with today, and boost biodiversity by making space for new vegetation, which would in turn attract grazing animals like deer. Indigenous peoples knew this well, and have a long history of using controlled burns to mimic the natural wildfire process. Indeed, they’ve been instrumental in getting fire agencies to do more of this kind of management lately.

In Washington, these researchers did their work with lidar, in which an airplane or drone fires lasers at the ground and measures what bounces back. Because scientists know the speed of light, a laser pulse traveling just a fraction of an inch farther comes back as a reading of a different elevation. The researchers first created a map of the area without snow, then another with the pack in place — combining the two provided an extremely detailed look at the depth of all that white stuff at any one spot. Lidar also mapped the tops of the trees, providing a snapshot of the vegetation. “It’s pretty neat — it’s almost like as if you drape a sheet over all of the trees,” Dickerson-Lange said. “So we could both look at how the canopy changed from forest thinning, as well as how the snow changed from forest thinning.” (Figuring out the density of the snow required a bit more work: The researchers had to actually visit the site and take samples.)

Previous research has found that depending on where you are on the landscape, a forest can either accelerate or delay the melting of its snow. Shade, for instance, will preserve it, but the trees also absorb and radiate solar energy, which melts it in unshaded spots. “That snow pack on the ground, even if it’s discontinuous in patches in between trees and the open gaps, it’s serving as a nice time-release storage mechanism of water,” said Daniel Swain, a climate scientist who studies weather and wildfires at University of California Agriculture and Natural Resources, but wasn’t involved in the research.

Overall, the new research finds that thinning this particular forest led to a net gain of snowpack. That’s because if the trees were crammed together, their crowns would intercept more snow, some of which would evaporate away without touching the ground. With more gaps, more snow can reach the forest floor and accumulate through the winter.

Still, scientists will have to do more research to figure out if the same goes for other regions — not only does every state have its own unique climates, but every forest is unique, too. And even different parts of the same forest will interact with snowpack in different ways. “This is why this is a very complex topic, and it’s very difficult to take the results from one site and try to generalize and apply that over a large spatial scale,” said Safeeq Khan, a hydrologist at the University of California, Merced, who wasn’t involved in the research but does similar experiments.

Interestingly, the researchers found a significant difference between the plots on the north and south slopes: While the former saw that boost of 30 percent, the latter saw about half that. Dickerson-Lange said that forest managers have tended to do more thinning on south-facing slopes, because they have a tendency to be drier and more prone to burn. “But these data suggest that there’s potential for greater water benefit to do heavier, more intense thinning on the north-facing slopes,” Dickerson-Lange added.

The urgency here is real, as climate change dramatically alters how much snow falls on the American west (the Cascades could lose half of its annual snowpack in the next 70 years), how long it lasts, and how much of the water eventually makes it to human populations. For researchers, then, snowpack is a rapidly moving target. “Eventually we are going to move to a point in a couple of decades where these snow-dominated ecosystems become rain-dominated, and now those dynamics are going to be different,” Swain said. “This winter is a great example of a year where, unfortunately, this effect was largely irrelevant, because really, there’s just no snow pack at that level at all.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on scientific research showing that forest thinning can increase snowpack accumulation, which in turn boosts water supply and reduces wildfire risk. This represents a meaningful environmental benefit with broad implications for water resources and wildfire management in the American West, addressing climate change challenges. The article provides detailed context on the study, its methods, and potential applications, making it a substantive and inspiring environmental breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: Thinning forests in Washington helps increase snowpack and water supply
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the western United States, snowpack in mountain forests is an important source of water for towns, farms, and cities. However, climate change is causing less snow and more rain, leading to less water stored in snow and increasing wildfire risks.

**What happened** – Scientists in Washington state’s Cascade Mountains tested how thinning trees affects snowpack. They cleared some trees to create more space between them and then measured how much snow accumulated on the ground compared to areas with dense trees. They used special laser technology called lidar to map tree heights and snow depth.

**Impact** – The study found that thinning trees by spacing them farther apart can increase snowpack by up to 30 percent. This means more water stored in snow during winter, which melts slowly and feeds rivers and streams. More snowpack helps provide extra water—about 4 million gallons more per 100 acres of forest—and can reduce the risk of large wildfires by clearing dry vegetation.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to test if these results apply to other forests and regions, since different forests and slopes behave differently. Forest managers may start thinning more on north-facing slopes to maximize water benefits. Continued study will help find the best ways to manage forests for both water supply and fire safety.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Carefully thinning trees in mountain forests can increase snowpack, boosting water supplies and lowering wildfire risks in a changing climate.
articles.personality_title_fr: Éclaircir les forêts en Washington augmente la neige et l'eau disponible
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans l'ouest des États-Unis, la neige dans les montagnes est une source importante d'eau pour les villes et les fermes. Mais le changement climatique provoque moins de neige et plus de pluie, ce qui réduit l'eau stockée sous forme de neige et augmente le risque d'incendies.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques dans les montagnes Cascade de l'État de Washington ont testé l'effet d'éclaircir les arbres sur la neige. Ils ont enlevé certains arbres pour créer plus d'espace entre eux, puis ont mesuré la quantité de neige au sol comparée aux zones où les arbres sont plus serrés. Ils ont utilisé une technologie laser appelée lidar pour cartographier la hauteur des arbres et la profondeur de la neige.

**Impact** – L'étude a montré que créer plus d'espace entre les arbres peut augmenter la neige jusqu'à 30 %. Cela signifie plus d'eau stockée sous forme de neige en hiver, qui fond lentement et nourrit les rivières. Plus de neige aide à fournir environ 4 millions de gallons d'eau supplémentaires pour 100 acres de forêt et réduit le risque de grands incendies en enlevant la végétation sèche.

**Étape suivante** – Les chercheurs veulent vérifier si ces résultats sont valables dans d'autres forêts et régions, car chaque forêt et pente réagit différemment. Les gestionnaires forestiers pourraient éclaircir davantage les pentes exposées au nord pour maximiser les bénéfices en eau. D'autres études aideront à mieux gérer les forêts pour l'eau et la sécurité contre les incendies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Éclaircir les arbres dans les forêts de montagne peut augmenter la neige, ce qui améliore l'eau disponible et réduit les risques d'incendies face au changement climatique.
articles.personality_title_es: Aclarear bosques en Washington aumenta la nieve y el suministro de agua
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el oeste de Estados Unidos, la nieve en las montañas es una fuente clave de agua para ciudades y cultivos. Pero el cambio climático está causando menos nieve y más lluvia, lo que reduce el agua almacenada en la nieve y aumenta el riesgo de incendios forestales.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos en las montañas Cascade de Washington probaron cómo afecta aclarar árboles a la acumulación de nieve. Quitaron algunos árboles para crear más espacio entre ellos y midieron cuánta nieve caía en esas áreas comparadas con zonas con árboles más juntos. Usaron tecnología láser llamada lidar para mapear la altura de los árboles y la profundidad de la nieve.

**Impacto** – El estudio encontró que aumentar el espacio entre árboles puede incrementar la nieve hasta en un 30 %. Esto significa más agua almacenada en la nieve durante el invierno, que luego alimenta ríos y arroyos. Más nieve ayuda a proporcionar aproximadamente 4 millones de galones adicionales de agua por cada 100 acres de bosque y reduce el riesgo de incendios grandes al eliminar vegetación seca.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren saber si estos resultados aplican a otros bosques y regiones, ya que cada lugar es diferente. Los encargados del bosque podrían aclarar más las laderas que miran al norte para obtener mayores beneficios de agua. Más estudios ayudarán a manejar mejor los bosques para el agua y la prevención de incendios.

**Resumen en una frase** – Aclarear árboles en bosques de montaña puede aumentar la nieve, mejorando el suministro de agua y disminuyendo el riesgo de incendios ante el cambio climático.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, detailed painting of a gently thinned Cascade forest on a sunlit north-facing slope, showing widely spaced ponderosa pines and Douglas firs with soft, thick snow accumulating evenly on the forest floor beneath their open canopies, while a crystal-clear stream flows nearby symbolizing abundant water supply, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and muted whites.
